coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots as additives in capillary electrophoresis for separation cinnamic acid and its derivatives
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Yaming
Bi, Qing
Zhang, Xiaoli
Wang, Litao
Zhang, Xia
Dong, Shuqing
Zhao, Liang
coredata.dc:description: A facile capillary electrophoresis (CE) method for the separation of cinnamic acid and its derivatives (3,4-dimethoxycinnamic acid, 4-methoxycinnamic acid, isoferulic acid, sinapic acid, cinnamic acid, ferulic acid, and trans-4-hydroxycinnamic acid) using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as additives with direct ultraviolet (UV) detection is reported. GQDs were synthesized by chemical oxidization and further purified by a macroporous resin column to remove salts (Na2SO4 and NaNO3) and other impurities. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) indicated that GQDs have a relatively uniform particle size (2.3 nm). Taking into account the structural features of GQDs, cinnamic acid and its derivatives were adopted as model compounds to investigate whether GQDs can be used to improve CE separations. The separation performance of GQDs used as additives in CE was studied through variations of pH, concentration of the background electrolyte (BGE), and contents of GQDs. The results indicated that excellent separation can be achieved in less than 18 min, which is mainly attributed to the interaction between the analytes and GQDs, especially isoferulic acid, sinapic acid, and cinnamic acid.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Additives
Capillary electrophoresis
Cinnamic acid and its derivatives

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of standard sample and GQDs modified BGE
Influence of pH and concentration of BGE
Influence of GQDs concentration and mechanism
Influence of separation voltage and injection time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structures of cinnamic acid and its derivatives.

(A) Preparation procedure of GQDs. (B) (a) TEM image of the as-prepared GQDs. The scale is 20 nm, and the inset photograph shows that the particle size distribution of GQDs is obtained by counting randomly 100 particles from various spots of the sample during TEM imaging. (B) (b) Magnified image of the as-prepared GQDs. The scale is 5 nm. (C) FTIR spectra of graphite (a) and GQDs (b). (D) GQDs modified BGE suspensions in different pH values.

(A) Electrochromatograms obtained for different pH values of 8.8 (a), 9.2 (b), 9.4 (c), and 9.6 (d). (B) Electrochromatograms obtained for different borate–NaOH buffer solution (pH 9.2, GQDs = 1.0 mg ml−1) concentrations of 10 mmol L−1 (a), 20 mmol L−1 (b), 30 mmol L−1 (c), 40 mmol L−1 (d), and 50 mmol L−1 (e). (C) Electrochromatograms obtained for 50 mmol L−1 borate–NaOH buffer solution (pH 9.2) containing different amounts of GQDs of 0 mg ml−1 (absence of GQDs) (a), 0.5 mg ml−1 (b), 0.8 mg ml−1 (c), 1.0 mg ml−1 (d), and 1.5 mg ml−1 (e). (D) Effect of concentration of GQDs on migration time of analytes (n = 3): (1) 3,4-dimethoxycinnamic acid; (2) 4-methoxycinnamic acid; (3) isoferulic acid; (4) sinapic acid; (5) cinnamic acid; (6) ferulic acid; (7) trans-4-hydroxycinnamic acid. Capillary column: 68.5 cm total length and 60 cm effective length; separation voltage: 15 kV; UV detection: 288 nm.
Mechanism of GQDs used as additives: (A) in the absence of GQDs; (B) 0.5–1.5 mg ml−1.
Separation of cinnamic acid and its derivatives under 50 mmol L−1 borate–NaOH buffer solution (pH 9.2) containing 1.0 mg ml−1 GQDs. Other conditions are the same as in Fig. 3.
Resolutions calculated for cinnamic acid and its derivatives in buffer solution containing different amounts of GQDs.

row.entry: Different amounts of GQDs in buffer solution

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note. 1: 3,4-Dimethoxycinnamic acid; 2: 4-methoxycinnamic acid; 3: isoferulic acid; 4: sinapic acid; 5: cinnamic acid; 6: ferulic acid; 7: trans-4-hydroxycinnamic acid.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Calibration curves, correlation coefficients, and LODs of cinnamic acid and its derivatives (n = 5).

row.entry: Regression equation a [y (10−4 AU); x (mg L−1)]

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: In the equations, y and x are the peak area (10−4 AU) and concentration of the analytes (mg L−1), respectively.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots as additives in capillary electrophoresis for separation cinnamic acid and its derivatives

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730000, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile capillary electrophoresis (CE) method for the separation of cinnamic acid and its derivatives (3,4-dimethoxycinnamic acid, 4-methoxycinnamic acid, isoferulic acid, sinapic acid, cinnamic acid, ferulic acid, and trans-4-hydroxycinnamic acid) using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as additives with direct ultraviolet (UV) detection is reported. GQDs were synthesized by chemical oxidization and further purified by a macroporous resin column to remove salts (Na2SO4 and NaNO3) and other impurities. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) indicated that GQDs have a relatively uniform particle size (2.3 nm). Taking into account the structural features of GQDs, cinnamic acid and its derivatives were adopted as model compounds to investigate whether GQDs can be used to improve CE separations. The separation performance of GQDs used as additives in CE was studied through variations of pH, concentration of the background electrolyte (BGE), and contents of GQDs. The results indicated that excellent separation can be achieved in less than 18 min, which is mainly attributed to the interaction between the analytes and GQDs, especially isoferulic acid, sinapic acid, and cinnamic acid.

ce:sections.ce:para: During the past decades, with the discovery of C60, carbon nanotubes, and most recently graphene, carbonaceous materials have received a great deal of interest [1]. In particular, graphene-based materials, consisting of two-dimensional single-atom carbon sheets, have been at the center of numerous investigations because of their outstanding physicochemical properties and widely used in various fields, including as molecular probing in living cells [2], a biosensor for electrochemical sensing [3], a pH sensor [4], a photovoltaic device [5], and a supercapacitor [6]. However, currently available graphene-based materials are produced by typical physical and chemical routes and are generally micrometer-sized graphene sheets [7].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new type of quantum dot and rising star in the graphene family, are nanometer-sized graphene sheets and consist of a single layer or a few layers of carbon atoms in a closely packed honeycomb structure [8], with a size less than 100 nm, and provide π–π electrostatic stacking interaction and hydrogen bonding sites. Meanwhile, in comparison with micrometer-sized graphene sheets, GQDs have well-confined shapes, are well water soluble, and have ultrahigh surface area-to-volume ratios, yielding high loading capacities with analytes. Most important, the reduction in size of GQDs has initiated a few different properties such as quantum size effects, edge effects [9], and stable photoluminescence [10]. Based on the many outstanding properties of GQDs, we believe that GQDs could be one of the most attractive and promising separating materials in the analytical science fields such as capillary electrophoresis (CE), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), gas chromatography (GC), and solid phase microextraction (SPME). However, up to now, most experimental works on GQDs have been mainly focused on optical and imaging analysis fields [7,11,12], and there have been few reports on GQDs used as separating materials. As an attractive and powerful separation technique, CE is often chosen for the determination of analytes because of its potential for high separation efficiency, short migration time, and minimal reagent consumption [13,14]. To improve the efficiency of separation, the use of various materials (e.g., silica nanoparticles [15,16], carbon nanotubes [17], grapheme [18], single-wall carbon nanotubes [19], gold nanoparticles [20], β-cyclodextrin [21–23]) as additives is probably the simple way. This is because this method does not require any procedure for the immobilization of nanoparticles onto the capillary. Moreover, the amount of materials introduced into the capillary can be easily controlled, completely avoiding the problems associated with the poor reproducibility between different capillary columns [24,25]. Currently, many articles have reported that a variety of materials are used as additives for CE to study their separation mechanism, especially carbon-based materials. Valcárcel and coworkers used single-walled carbon nanohorns as additives for separation water-soluble vitamins in CE and obtained good separation [17]. Subsequently, they also used graphene nanoparticles as additives for improving the electrokinetic separation of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. Based on the precedents of nanoparticles as powerful analytical tools in CE as additives and the above-mentioned excellent properties of GQDs, we believe that GQDs as additives in CE could exhibit great advantages, such as yielding high loading capacities with analytes, and GQDs introduced into the capillary could be easily fulfilled. To the best of our knowledge, there are few reports of work of CE separation using GQDs as additives.

To investigate the possibilities of their application in CE and the mechanism for improving analyte separation of GQDs, cinnamic acid and its derivatives (3,4-dimethoxycinnamic acid, 4-methoxycinnamic acid, isoferulic acid, sinapic acid, cinnamic acid, ferulic acid, and trans-4-hydroxycinnamic acid; structures are given in Fig. 1 ) were adopted as model compounds. This is mainly because these compounds, which have aromatic rings and hydrogen bonding sites, could interact with GQDs through π–π electrostatic stacking interaction, preferably having hydrogen bonding sites. In addition, these interactions could introduce changes in the electrophoretic velocity of the analytes and improve the selectivity of separation. Another reason is that these seven compounds have similar structures, and it was difficult to achieve good separation (especially isoferulic acid, sinapic acid, and cinnamic acid) by CE. Furthermore, these seven compounds widely existed in many traditional Chinese medicines, and they were not only important as stomachic, astringent, carminative, and antidiabetic agents but also displayed many pharmacological properties such as antioxidant, antimicrobial activity, and even prevention of cancer and possessed important clinical application value [26,27].

In this study, we successfully synthesized GQDs, which have a relatively uniform particle size with 2.3 nm by chemical oxidization and were further purified by a macroporous resin column to remove salts (Na2SO4 and NaNO3) and other impurities. To research the possibilities of the application of GQDs in CE and develop the mechanism for improving separation efficiency of analytes, we report a simple CE method for the separation of cinnamic acid and its derivatives using GQDs as additives with direct ultraviolet (UV) detection. The separation performances of GQDs as additives in CE were studied through variation of pH, concentration of the background electrolyte (BGE), and contents of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: All reagents used were of analytical grade unless otherwise stated. Graphite was purchased from Qingdao Nanshu Hongda Graphite Products (Qingdao, China). The macroporous resin was purchased from Sunresin New Materials (Xi'an, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Beijing, China). Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), nitric acid (HNO3), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent (Chengdu, China). HPLC-grade methanol (MeOH) was purchased from Shanghai First Reagent Factory (Shanghai, China). Disodium tetraborate decahydrate (Na2B4O7•10H2O) and potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) were obtained from Xi'an Chemical Reagent (Xi'an, China). Cinnamic acid and its derivatives were purchased from Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). All chemicals were used without any further purification, and all aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water.

The capillary electrophoretic separations were performed using a Beckman Coulter P/ACE MDQ CE system (Fullerton, CA, USA) in conjunction with a UV detector operated at 288 nm unless stated otherwise. Data collection and system operation were controlled by version 7.0 32 Karat software (Beckman Coulter). The bare fused silica capillary (75 μm i.d. and 375 μm o.d) was purchased from Yongnian Photoconductive Fiber Factory (Hebei Province, China). A capillary with a total length of 68.5 cm and an effective length of 60 cm was used in all capillary electrophoretic experiments. Prior to the first use each day, the capillary was successively rinsed by methanol (15 min), ultrapure water (20 min), 0.1 mol L−1 HCl (15 min), ultrapure water (20 min), 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH (15 min), ultrapure water (20 min), and running buffer (10 min). Between each separation, the capillary was rinsed sequentially with 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH (10 min), ultrapure water (10 min), and running buffer (10 min).
The microstructure of graphene quantum dots was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Philips Tecnai-12, 120 kV).
Infrared spectrum was measured with a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (IFS120HR, Bruker).

GQDs were synthesized from flake graphite powder by modified chemical oxidization [28] and purified by the macroporous resin column to remove salts and other impurities (process is given in Fig. 2 A). Briefly, 1.5 g of flake graphite powder was refluxed with a 120 ml mixture of concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3 (H2SO4/HNO3 = 5:1) at 130 °C for 30 h. Then, heating was stopped and the reaction system was cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, NaOH was added to the resultant solution to adjust the pH to neutral. Finally, the resultant solution was put on the macroporous resin column to remove Na2SO4, NaNO3, and other impurities, and a reddish-brown solid was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of standard sample and GQDs modified BGE
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of the individual cinnamic acid and its derivative stock solutions was prepared at 1 mg ml−1 concentration in methanol (HPLC grade) and stored at 4 °C. Each peak in the electropherogram was able to validate its identification with the single sample spiked.
Stock solution of a certain concentration of GQDs suspension (1.5 mg ml−1) was prepared by suspending GQDs in ultrapure water.

Stock solution of 50 mmol L−1 borate buffer solution modified by GQDs was prepared by dissolving a certain amount of disodium tetraborate decahydrate in GQDs suspension (1.5 mg ml−1), and it was adjusted to the appropriate pH value with 50 mmol L−1 NaOH or 100 mmol L−1 phosphate buffer solution.
Stock solution of 100 mmol L−1 phosphate buffer solution modified by GQDs was prepared by dissolving a certain amount of potassium dihydrogen phosphate in GQDs suspension (1.5 mg ml−1).
Stock solution of 50 mmol L−1 NaOH modified by GQDs was prepared by dissolving a certain amount of sodium hydroxide in GQDs suspension (1.5 mg ml−1).
Before use, all solutions were filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous nylon membrane and ultrasonicated.

GQDs were synthesized following a one-step chemical oxidization procedure and further purified by macroporous resin column as shown in Fig. 2A. The morphology of GQDs was confirmed by TEM images (Fig. 2B(a) and B(b)), and it can be seen that GQDs have a relatively uniform particle size. The distribution of the diameter sizes of GQDs was evaluated statistically through measuring the diameter of 100 GQDs in the selected TEM images. It is noted that the diameter sizes of GQDs were distributed mainly between 1 and 5 nm, with an average diameter of approximately 2.3 nm (Fig. 2B(a), inset).

FTIR spectra for graphite and GQDs are shown in Fig. 2C (curves a and b, respectively). For GQDs (curve b), the characteristic peaks at 3330 and 1661 cm−1 are assigned to O–H stretching vibrations and the CC stretching bands for aromatic rings. All of the spectra of the graphene derivatives have the two characteristic bands. The band around 1729 cm−1 corresponds to CO stretching vibrations from carbonyl and carboxylic groups. The bands around 1450 cm−1 are attributed to C–OH stretching vibrations. The FTIR spectra provide evidence of the presence of different types of oxygen functionalities on GQDs material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH and concentration of BGE

ce:section.ce:para: The pH of BGE is an important factor affecting the separation of analytes because the ionization of analytes depends on such a pH; meanwhile, the pH value could also change the protonation degrees of GQDs that normally carry a negative charge. Thus, the pH value could affect the interaction between GQDs and analytes, and a suitable pH value could result in the best separation efficiency. We investigated the influence of the pH of BGE by formulating a series of pH 8.8 to 9.6 buffer solutions at a buffer solution concentration of 50 mmol L−1 and GQDs concentration of 1 mg ml−1. As shown in Fig. 3 A, it was found that when the pH of BGE was 9.2, isoferulic acid, sinapic acid, and cinnamic acid could be separated completely, whereas they could not be separated completely in other pH conditions. When the pH value increased, the migration time of analytes should theoretically decrease, but there was little actual change; this may be because the variation of migration time is the combined effect of pH value and GQDs. Whereas the migration time decreased as the pH value increased, the interaction between GQDs and analytes, which made the mass-to-charge ratio of analytes become large, led to the migration time of analytes becoming longer. The degree of change of migration time caused by GQDs and pH offset each other; thus, the migration time of analytes had little actual change.

It is well known that the most important condition of nanoparticles using additives for CE is being capable of forming a stable dispersion solution in BGE. Although GQDs are strongly hydrophilic and water soluble, the extent of GQDs reunion in the BGE must be investigated. A lot of previous studies indicated that the graphene oxide sheet aggregated in using the higher concentration salt solution [29]; especially in the presence of an electric field, the strong aggregation will take place. As such, GQDs may also occur in this phenomenon. Moreover, this strong aggregation phenomenon will cause the blockage of the capillary column in the separation process. Thus, we investigated the extent of GQDs reunion in the BGE by formulating a series of pH 5.0 to 10.0 buffer solutions at a concentration of 50 mmol L−1 and a GQDs concentration of 1.5 mg ml−1. From Fig. 2D, we found that the aggregation of GQDs was invisible and that all GQDs modifying BGEs were stable suspensions, even in a week. Furthermore, when all GQDs modified BGEs were filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous nylon membrane, GQDs were not trapped in the membrane. Compared with carbon nanotubes [17] or graphene [18], which need surfactant or ultrasonication in the separation process to obtain stable suspensions, GQDs could be used directly as additives without surfactant or ultrasonication, even under high-salt conditions or in the presence of an electric field.

In addition, the concentration of BGE also is a key parameter to influence the separation. The effect of the concentration of BGE on these separations was examined at the BGE concentrations of 10–50 mmol L−1, pH 9.2, and GQDs concentration of 1 mg ml−1 (Fig. 3B). From Fig. 3B, experimental results showed that the best separation for cinnamic acid and its derivatives could be obtained at 50 mmol L−1 of BGE. With the increase of BGE concentration, the retention time of cinnamic acid and its derivatives increased, and these separations were much improved; in particular, the resolutions of isoferulic acid, sinapic acid, and cinnamic acid increased. The most likely reason is that increasing BGE concentration is equivalent to increasing the ionic strength, resulting in reducing the electric double layer thickness and decreasing electroosmotic flow (EOF), which led to the increase of migration time. So, the increased migration time could provide the maximum interaction between GQDs and analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of GQDs concentration and mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: When GQDs were added to the BGE used as additives, under certain electrophoretic conditions the analytes were separated according to their effective charge and also by their particular interactions that contain π–π electrostatic stacking interaction, hydrogen bonding, or electrostatic interaction with GQDs. These additional partitioning effects between GQDs and the analytes could increase the number of separation degrees of freedom and enable the separation of analytes [30]. Therefore, the concentration of GQDs is an important factor in achieving the separation of cinnamic acid and its derivatives.

To confirm the effect of GQDs on the separation of cinnamic acid and its derivatives, the effect of the concentration of GQDs on these separations was examined at concentrations of 0–1.5 mg ml−1 (Fig. 3C). In the absence of GQDs, from Fig. 3C, it can be seen that the good separation of cinnamic acid and its derivatives cannot be achieved; in particular, isoferulic acid and sinapic acid are completely unable to achieve the separation, and there is no resolution for isoferulic acid and sinapic acid in Table 1 . When the concentration of GQDs was increased from 0 to 1 mg ml−1, the separation of cinnamic acid and its derivatives got better and better in Fig. 3C, and these resolutions were gradually improved in Table 1. However, when the contents of GQDs were increased from 1 to 1.5 mg ml−1, the resolutions of the first five analytes appeared to worsen, although resolution between compounds 5 and 6 and between compounds 6 and 7 improved. Overall, it seems that they achieved the best separation at a GQDs concentration of 1 mg ml−1; in particular, isoferulic acid, sinapic acid. and cinnamic acid obtained a baseline separation, so the best results in terms of resolution and sensitivity were obtained with 1 mg ml−1 GQDs. In addition, Fig. 3D shows the migration time of the analytes at different GQDs concentrations in the BGE. It is shown that the GQDs modified BGE led to more migration time than the GQDs unmodified BGE. These experimental results showed that cinnamic acid derivatives and GQDs occurred in some interactions, and these interactions influenced their mobility, resulting in their separations. The separation mechanism may be that the cinnamic acid and its derivatives that have aromatic characteristics interacted with GQDs through π–π electrostatic stacking interaction or hydrogen bonding interaction, and the increased mass-to-charge ratio of the analytes resulted in the migration slowdown (Fig. 4 A and B). In addition, the degree of change of migration time resulting from these interactions was different for each compound. Thus, cinnamic acid and its derivatives got the better separation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of separation voltage and injection time
ce:section.ce:para: The separation efficiency of CE was investigated within the separation voltage range from 12 to 20 kV according to our previous studies [31]. The migration time of the analytes was significantly shortened, and their corresponding current peaks were sharpened when the separation voltage was increased. However, if the separation voltage was too high, more Joule heat was produced because of the higher current inside the capillary, which caused peak broadening and reduced separation efficiency [32]. For a comprehensive thought, 15 kV was selected as the optimal separation voltage in this experiment.

The separation efficiency of CE was investigated within the injection time range from 3 to 10 s. We found that the peak current increased as the injection time was prolonged. However, the current peaks of the analytes were obviously broadened and overlapped when the injection time was more than 8 s. So, 5 s was selected as the injection time in this experiment, and satisfactory results were obtained under this condition.

Through the experiments above, the optimal conditions of CE for determining the seven analytes were separation voltage of 15 kV, 20 °C column temperature, pressure injection time of 5 s (pressure = 0.5 psi), and 50 mmol L−1 borate–NaOH buffer (pH 9.2) with 1 mg ml−1 GQDs. Fig. 5 shows electropherograms of the seven analytes under the optimal conditions. It is clear that a very good baseline separation was achieved for seven analytes within 18 min.

The calibration curves of cinnamic acid and its derivatives with GQDs as additives were constructed by using a series of the standard mixture solutions over a range of 20–500 mg L−1 for each compound. Table 2 summarizes the analytical parameters obtained with GQDs (1.0 mg ml−1) as additives. These results obtained were good, with correlation coefficients ranging between 0.9901 and 0.9994, which showed good linearity. Limits of detection (LODs) using a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3 were obtained. As shown in Table 2, the LODs of this method ranged from 11.7 to 41.8 mg L−1.

The repeatability of the proposed method was assessed in terms of peak area and migration time from five independent measurements of solutions of cinnamic acid and its derivatives, which have the same concentration (mg ml−1) in the same day under the selected optimal conditions. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) of peak area and migration time, respectively, were 3.32 and 2.70% for 3,4-dimethoxycinnamic acid, 3.25 and 3.08% for 4-methoxycinnamic acid, 3.35 and 1.49% for isoferulic acid, 3.82 and 1.24% for sinapic acid, 4.13 and 1.53% for cinnamic acid, 3.25 and 1.44% for ferulic acid, and 3.09 and 1.41% for trans-4-hydroxycinnamic acid (n = 5). The high repeatability indicates that the system is stable and this method is dependable.

We have reported a simple CE method for the separation of cinnamic acid and its derivatives using GQDs as additives with direct UV detection. The important factors known to affect separation efficiency, such as variation of pH, concentration of BGE, and contents of GQDs, were systematically investigated. Under optimal separation conditions, the resolution of cinnamic acid and its derivatives increased, especially isoferulic acid, sinapic acid, and cinnamic acid. This may be mainly due to the structural characteristics of GQDs offering a lot of interaction sites such as hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction and π–π electrostatic stacking interaction. These interactions could introduce changes in the electrophoretic velocity of the analytes and improve the selectivity of the separation when GQDs interacted with the analytes. Combining their own structural characteristics and physicochemical properties of GQDs with the better separation of GQDs used as additives in CE, we can foresee that GQDs will be a promising separation material in CE, HPLC, GC, and extraction separation technology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405161, 21405162, and 20675085).
